title: Shop-Made Corner Clamp
summary: A slotted plywood corner clamp that flexes to squeeze a mitered frame joint closed instead of just pinning the corner.
description:
Most improvised corner clamps just pin a joint at 90° without applying any closing pressure across the miter itself — this one is built specifically to squeeze the two mitered faces together, which is what actually closes a gap in a loose-fitting miter.

Slots cut into the plywood base let the jaw sections **flex inward** when you tighten the wing nuts, drawing the two sides of the joint together rather than just holding them in place. That flex is the entire mechanism — there's no moving hardware beyond the bolts themselves.

**Orient the counterbored bolt slot on the jaw blocks facing down, against the base** — mounting it facing up is an easy mistake that a previous builder ran into, and it keeps the bolt heads from interfering with the clamping action.
timeLabel: 45 min–1.25 hrs plus glue drying time

tools.note: Holds the jaw blocks in place while their glue cures.
Cuts the flex slots in the base.

cutList.note: Slotted to let the jaw sections flex inward.
Angled face; counterbored bolt slot faces DOWN against the base.

steps.title: Cut and slot the base
steps.body: Cut the base 6" x 10" from the 1/2" plywood, squaring all four edges — this is the spring of the whole tool, so a base cut out of square puts the jaws out of square with it. Then cut the flex slots into it on the table saw. What the slots have to achieve is that the jaw-carrying sections of the base can bend inward under bolt pressure instead of sitting rigid: stop each slot short of the outer edges so the base stays one piece, and lay them out symmetrically so both sections flex by the same amount. If one side flexes further than the other it will drag the miter out of alignment as you tighten, which defeats the point of building this instead of using a pinning-style corner jig.

steps.title: Glue on the jaw blocks
steps.body: Glue the four hardwood jaw blocks — 3/4" x 1-1/2" x 3", each with an angled face — onto the base with their angled faces turned in toward where the mitered corner will sit, so they bear flat against the two workpieces instead of on a point. Every block goes on with its counterbored bolt slot facing DOWN against the base, not up; a previous builder got this backwards, and face-up puts the bolt head exactly where it fouls the closing action. Clamp each block with a bar clamp while the glue cures and watch that it doesn't slide out of position as the clamp tightens — a block that creeps takes the jaw's angle with it. Let the glue cure fully before drilling; a green glue joint will break out when the bit exits.

steps.title: Drill for the bolts
steps.body: Drill one hole through each jaw block and the base beneath it for the 1/4" carriage bolts, four holes in total, centred in each block's counterbored slot. Size the holes so the bolts drop through freely rather than gripping the shank — a tight hole locks the base's slotted sections and kills the flex the whole tool depends on. Back the plywood with a scrap board so the bit doesn't blow out the underside as it breaks through, since the underside is the face that bears against the bench.

steps.title: Assemble
steps.body: Run a carriage bolt through each of the four holes with a washer at each end — eight washers for four bolts — so neither the bolt head nor the wing nut crushes into the plywood as it tightens. Thread the wing nuts on finger tight for now. Check the stack-up: the 1/2" base plus a 3/4" jaw block is 1-1/4", which leaves the 2" bolt about 3/4" of length for the two washers and the wing nut, so there is enough thread but not a lot of spare — if a wing nut runs out of thread before it bites, you have a washer too many somewhere.

steps.title: Use it
steps.body: Glue both mitered faces, set the two pieces into the clamp with the corner pushed up against the jaw blocks, and tighten the wing nuts a little at a time, alternating between them rather than running one down fully first. As they tighten, the slotted sections of the base flex inward and squeeze the two mitered faces together — that closing pressure across the joint, not the 90° registration, is what actually shuts the gap in a loose-fitting miter. Confirm the corner is still square once it is under full pressure, wipe the squeeze-out before it skins over, and leave it clamped until the glue has cured.
